title: FBI: Fragile Binary Interface problem
description: This section explains the Fragile Binary Interface (FBI) problem and the solutions offered by languages ​​such as Swift/ObjC regarding ABI stability and compilation speed.
content:
## Background

Recently, I was reading about the history of macOS, and it mentioned Apple's longest migration, 64-bit support. It discussed a huge challenge that object-oriented languages ​​(OOP) like Objective-C face at the system level (or software level): the Fragile Binary Interface (FBI).

## FBI

FBI stands for Fragile Binary Interface Problem. In Chinese, it refers to the problem of resilient binary interfaces.

### Example

The Fragile Binary Interface Problem is a common issue in object-oriented programming languages. If an external library is introduced into a program, and our program uses and inherits classes from that library, and if the external library is modified, we must recompile the entire inheritance tree of those classes. This type of problem is called a fragile base class. The so-called fragile interface is a universal problem in modern programming languages; its full name is Fragile Binary Interface.

In simple terms, in Swift and most mainstream programming languages, class properties are accessed via offsets at the underlying level. For example, `book.name`. Accessing an object's property in the program is very intuitive. However, after compilation, it's actually in the form of `location(book) + offset(name)`. Accessing the `name` property is achieved by adding an offset to the memory address of the `book` object. In Swift, after compilation, the offset values ​​of all its properties are calculated. This speeds up runtime execution because these offsets don't need to be calculated at runtime.

If there were no relationships between classes, there wouldn't be any problems. However, in practice, we use classes with inheritance relationships. If our main project references an external library, and our main program inherits classes from that library, then if there are any changes to that library, we must recompile the code in the entire inheritance tree. Otherwise, offset calculation errors will occur in the subclasses of our main program. In short, if Swift can handle the Fragile Binary Interface well, the most direct benefit for developers is faster compilation speed.

### Solution

The approach starts with offset tables.

1. .NET uses intermediate files to list offsets and other information from the compilation phase, creating metadata. The linker then uses this metadata to self-correct when loading the library into the application.

2. C++ determines location dependencies by inserting placeholder objects (this is a general overview of the process) in case additional functionality is needed later. The cost is increased memory usage.

3. Objective-C 2.0 (for 64-bit Mac OS X) provides non-fragile, extensible instance variables as intermediate access.

It's clear that these solutions address compilation issues through intermediate layers.

For more details, see the wiki: [Fragile binary interface problem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fragile_binary_interface_problem)
